# Consult (design-partner): LOCK the final 5 golden questions — hardest + representative-for-DIFFERENTIATION, bias-free (I-safety-002b / #925)

Operator: "give me 5 questions that are truly golden AND hardest AND representative to show differentiation." Your prior verdict: A (clinical-3 #75/76/78 + robustness #62/#90), with the hard pushback that "pick the hardest" is selection bias unless pre-registered from an external criterion. Now add the "representative to show differentiation" requirement WITHOUT re-introducing bias.

## My reconciliation (critique it)
"Representative to show differentiation" must NOT mean "questions where POLARIS wins" (that's the bias you flagged). The principled, pre-registerable criterion = golden + hardest + **high-stakes AND citation-faithfulness-critical** — i.e. questions where a fabricated/unsupported citation is dangerous or where the task itself demands citation quality. POLARIS's differentiator is provable per-claim faithfulness; the fair way to "show differentiation" is to test on the question CLASS where faithfulness matters most, not to pick questions POLARIS happens to win.

On that criterion I think **#62 (ion-trap quantum scaling) is the weak link** — hard, but it's an off-turf STEM control where citation-faithfulness isn't the crux (you yourself called it a "harsh off-turf control"). I'd swap it for **#72** (AI labor-market literature review — the task EXPLICITLY requires "only high-quality, English-language journal articles," so citation quality/faithfulness IS the literal deliverable → directly differentiation-representative + golden + hard).

## Proposed final 5 (all DeepResearch Bench EN, golden)
- #75 — plasma metal-ion modulation for CVD prevention/therapy + clinical evidence (clinical, high-stakes)
- #76 — gut microbiota / probiotics / pathogen toxic metabolites / diet (clinical, high-stakes)
- #78 — Parkinson's post-DBS warning signs + support (clinical, high-stakes)
- #72 — AI labor-market lit review, high-quality journals only (citation-faithfulness IS the task)
- #90 — ADAS liability: ADAS principles + legal frameworks + case law + regulatory recs (high-stakes, case-law-citation-sensitive)

## Questions for you
1. Is swapping #62 -> #72 correct for "representative to show differentiation" without bias? Or does #72 (lit-review) tilt unfairly toward POLARIS (which is built to cite)? Is that tilt legitimate (it's the differentiator) or a bias?
2. Is the SELECTION RULE defensible: "golden DRB-EN + (all eligible hard clinical) + fill remaining from hard high-stakes-citation-critical tasks by a pre-registered criterion"? State the exact rule so it's pre-registerable + reproducible.
3. Confirm #72, #75, #76, #78, #90 are all in DRB-EN and are hardest-tier.
4. Any of the 5 that is NOT defensible? Any better hardest-tier DRB-EN task for the "high-stakes citation-critical" slots (e.g. #54 finance, #90 law, #81 history)?
5. Final honest framing/label for this exact 5 (so we don't overclaim).

## Output
Design-partner prose, specific. Lock the 5 + the selection rule + the honest label. Push back if my #62->#72 swap is wrong.

.\docs\clinical_rag_validation_sota_2026_05_26.md:15:**Pivot recommendation for the 9-day window: do NOT rip out the regex+atom stack. Bolt on three things, in this order:** (1) **Vectara HHEM-2.1-Open** (0.1B params, Apache-2.0, ~0.6s/section on RTX 3090, self-hostable, catches negation correctly — see §3.2) as a per-section faithfulness gate post-strict-verify; (2) **Patronus Lynx 8B** (open source, MIT/Llama-3 license, beats GPT-4o on PubMedQA by 8.3%, returns JSON `{REASONING, SCORE: PASS|FAIL}`) as a per-section LLM-judge gate; (3) Keep the current atom system as the **generation-time scaffold** but stop treating it as the verification layer — it's the prompt-design layer. Codex's §-1.1 line-by-line audit becomes the gold-standard third gate (already in place). Demo-ready by 2026-06-02 with ~400 lines of plumbing, no architecture rip-out.
.\docs\clinical_rag_validation_sota_2026_05_26.md:55:**Latency.** ~0.6s for 4096-token context on consumer RTX 3090; ~1.5s on Intel Xeon CPU [^hhem_blog]. For POLARIS: a typical generated section is ~20 sentences × ~80 tokens/sentence + ~200 tokens of cited span per claim → ~3000 tokens per HHEM call × 20 calls = ~12s per section if serialised, or sub-second if batched on a single GPU pass.
.\docs\clinical_rag_validation_sota_2026_05_26.md:63:**Demo-timeline fit.** **Best of any option in this report.** A 200-line Python module wrapping HHEM-2.1 as a per-section gate (load model once at startup, batch all section sentences, threshold at 0.5 for hard fail and 0.7 for warning) is a 1-day implementation. The model file is ~600MB; first-load latency ~5s.
.\docs\clinical_rag_validation_sota_2026_05_26.md:95:**Negation handling.** **Mixed.** The arxiv 2510.20375 paper "The Impact of Negated Text on Hallucination" shows LLMs broadly struggle with negation, but NLI-style entailment models — by virtue of being trained on MNLI's contradiction class — have a structural advantage: a "did not occur" hypothesis paired with "occurred at 0.4%" premise is exactly the contradiction-class training signal [^negation_paper_2025]. HHEM-2.1 is itself an NLI-derived model trained on RAG-specific data and inherits this advantage (see §3.2).
.\docs\clinical_rag_validation_sota_2026_05_26.md:97:**Production status.** DeBERTa-v3-large-mnli is 435M params, runs ~150ms/pair on RTX 3090. HALT-RAG (arxiv 2509.07475) packages calibrated NLI ensembles with abstention as a production framework [^halt_rag_2025].
.\docs\clinical_rag_validation_sota_2026_05_26.md:107:**MiniCheck (the production-ready option).** 770M Flan-T5-Large, sentence-level fact-checker, **matches GPT-4 on LLM-AggreFact (74.7% vs 75.3%, 0.6% gap) at 400× lower cost** [^minicheck_2024]. Available on GitHub. **However: the paper explicitly states "we disregard the usual 'contradiction' class from textual entailment, as contradictions are rare in our benchmark"** — this means MiniCheck will NOT reliably catch the constipation negation fabrication, because it treats faithfulness as binary support/no-support, not three-way support/neutral/contradiction. **Disqualifier for POLARIS clinical use case.**
.\docs\clinical_rag_validation_sota_2026_05_26.md:127:**Verdict.** **Use MedHELM + MedHallu as evaluation gates pre-demo** (score POLARIS on a sample, include the score in the Carney pitch deck — "POLARIS scores X on MedHallu hardest category"). Don't try to embed MedHallu detection methodology in the runtime — HHEM and Lynx cover that lane more cleanly.
.\docs\clinical_rag_validation_sota_2026_05_26.md:192:1. **Issue: I-rag-001 — HHEM-2.1 gate** (1 day). Wrap `vectara/hallucination_evaluation_model` HF model. Per section: for each generated sentence + cited span, compute FCS. Threshold: <0.5 = hard fail (replace with refusal), 0.5-0.7 = log + emit warning, >0.7 = pass. Add to `src/polaris_graph/evaluator/`. Sovereignty-compatible — runs on the same OVH BHS5 node as the rest of POLARIS.
.\docs\clinical_rag_validation_sota_2026_05_26.md:194:2. **Issue: I-rag-002 — Lynx 8B gate** (2 days). Wrap `PatronusAI/Llama-3-Patronus-Lynx-8B-Instruct` HF model. Per section block: send (question, document, generated-section-text) to Lynx, parse JSON `{REASONING, SCORE}`. SCORE=FAIL → section replaced with refusal + REASONING logged to evaluator_rule_checks.json. Latency budget: ~5s/section on RTX 3090 (acceptable).
.\docs\clinical_rag_validation_sota_2026_05_26.md:196:3. **Issue: I-rag-003 — pre-demo benchmark on MedHallu sample** (1 day). Run 100-sample MedHallu subset through the augmented pipeline. Report F1 on hallucination detection. Include result in Carney pitch deck: "POLARIS scores X on MedHallu's hardest category (Incomplete Information, the category that defeats raw GPT-4o)."
.\docs\clinical_rag_validation_sota_2026_05_26.md:202:**Expected outcome.** Fabrication rate drops from ~3.7% (current) to <1% (HHEM + Lynx both flag the constipation case; either one catches it suffices). Refusal rate stays around 30-45% (V4 Pro's pathology, not the validator's). MedHallu F1 should land in 0.75-0.85 range based on Lynx-70B's published 0.83 on PubMedQA.

.\docs\clinical_rag_validation_sota_2026_05_26.md:355:[^gcite_pcite_2025]: arxiv 2509.21557, [Generation-Time vs. Post-hoc Citation: A Holistic Evaluation of LLM Attribution](https://arxiv.org/html/2509.21557) — verbatim: "We recommend a retrieval-centric, P-Cite-first approach for high-stakes applications, reserving G-Cite for precision-critical settings such as strict claim verification." P-Cite achieves 78% vs G-Cite 69% answer correctness; P-Cite 37% vs G-Cite 41% hallucination rate.
.\docs\clinical_rag_validation_sota_2026_05_26.md:359:[^hhem_blog]: Vectara blog, [HHEM 2.1: A Better Hallucination Detection Model](https://www.vectara.com/blog/hhem-2-1-a-better-hallucination-detection-model). Latency: 0.6s on RTX 3090, 1.5s on Intel Xeon w7-3445 (4096 tokens). "1.5x better than GPT-3.5-Turbo on RAGTruth's Summarization and QA subsets, and over 30% (relative) better than GPT-4."
.\docs\clinical_rag_validation_sota_2026_05_26.md:367:[^negation_paper_2025]: arxiv 2510.20375, [The Impact of Negated Text on Hallucination with Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2510.20375). LLMs struggle with negation; NLI-derived models have structural advantage via MNLI contradiction class.

.\outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_4\findings.md:20:The main correctness gap is the unbounded `worker.join()` at `live_retriever.py:304`. AccessBypass has many internal timeouts, including Crawl4AI `wait_for(..., timeout=timeout_seconds + 10)` (`access_bypass.py:673-678`) and aiohttp timeouts, but not every failure mode is covered: browser startup (`__aenter__`), browser cleanup (`__aexit__`), imports, native extension work, or subprocess wedges can still block the worker. .\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_5\codex_stderr.log:469:A meta-analysis of trials in patients with obesity or type 2 diabetes found tirzepatide induced a mean weight reduction of 10.39 kg versus placebo (95% CI: –10.80 to –9.99).[8] In a related obesity trial without diabetes, SURMOUNT-5, a phase 3 trial comparing tirzepatide 15 mg to semaglutide 2.4 mg, the mean percentage change in body weight was a reduction of 20.2% with tirzepatide compared with 13.7% for semaglutide.[9] That same trial reported patients treated with tirzepatide were 1.3, 1.6, 1.8, and 2 times more likely to have at least 10%, 15%, 20%, and 25% weight loss, respectively, compared to semaglutide.[9] A cost-effectiveness analysis based on the SURPASS-2 trial in patients with type 2 diabetes found tirzepatide had a lower cost per responder than semaglutide 1 mg for achieving HbA1c < 5.7% (all doses) and weight loss ≥10% (all doses).[10] The number needed to treat (NNT) for all doses of tirzepatide were statistically significantly lower than that for semaglutide 1 mg to achieve weight loss endpoints of ≥5%, ≥10%, and ≥15%.[10] In that substudy, tirzepatide resulted in a 33.9% reduction in fat mass versus 8.2% with placebo and a 10.9% reduction in lean mass versus 2.6% with placebo at week 72.[11] A post-hoc analysis of the SURMOUNT trials reported that treatment-emergent adverse events potentially related to macronutrient malnutrition, such as abnormal loss of weight and underweight, were uncommon, occurring in 0.12% of tirzepatide-treated participants.[12] The same analysis found 0.38% of tirzepatide-treated participants reached a BMI < 18.5 kg/m².[12] For safety comparisons, the meta-analysis reported tirzepatide was associated with increased risks of nausea, diarrhea, and vomiting compared to placebo, while aggregated data indicated no increased risk of headache (OR=1.00, 95%CI:0.84 to 1.20).[8]

.\scripts\audit_dashboard_visual.py:98:    {"type": "llm_call", "call_type": "section_write", "section_id": "s1", "word_count": 1250, "evidence_count": 12, "title": "PFAS Adsorption Mechanisms and Chain-Length Effects", "content": "## PFAS Adsorption Mechanisms and Chain-Length Effects\n\nGranular activated carbon (GAC) remains the most widely deployed technology for per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) removal from drinking water, primarily operating through hydrophobic interactions between fluorinated carbon chains and the activated carbon surface [1]. Pilot-scale studies demonstrate that GAC achieves **93.2% removal of PFOS** and **91.8% removal of PFOA** at standard empty bed contact times (EBCT) of 10-20 minutes [1].\n\n### Chain-Length Dependence\n\nThe effectiveness of GAC adsorption is strongly dependent on PFAS chain length. Long-chain compounds (C8+) such as PFOS and PFOA exhibit significantly higher adsorption due to stronger hydrophobic driving forces, routinely achieving >90% removal [2]. In contrast, short-chain PFAS compounds (C4-C6) show dramatically reduced removal efficiency:\n\n- **PFBA (C4):** 32% removal\n- **PFHxA (C6):** 47% removal  \n- **PFOS (C8):** >90% removal [2]\n\nThis chain-length dependence has critical implications for treatment plant design, as contamination profiles increasingly include short-chain PFAS from industrial substitution of legacy long-chain compounds.\n\n### Natural Organic Matter Competition\n\nA significant operational challenge is competitive adsorption by natural organic matter (NOM). In source waters with elevated total organic carbon (TOC > 4 mg/L), GAC bed life for PFAS removal decreases from approximately 18 months to 7-11 months, representing a **40-60% reduction** in effective service life [1]. This competition mechanism must be accounted for in treatment system sizing and media replacement scheduling.", "vid": "PG_AUDIT_001", "ts": "2026-02-26T10:03:30Z"},
.\scripts\audit_dashboard_visual.py:99:    {"type": "llm_call", "call_type": "section_write", "section_id": "s2", "word_count": 980, "evidence_count": 9, "title": "Ion Exchange Resins: Performance and Cost Trade-offs", "content": "## Ion Exchange Resins: Performance and Cost Trade-offs\n\nPFAS-selective ion exchange (IX) resins represent the primary alternative to GAC for drinking water treatment, offering superior removal of both long and short-chain PFAS compounds. PFAS-selective IX resins demonstrate **97.3% total PFAS removal** across all chain lengths, compared to GAC's variable performance that degrades significantly for short-chain compounds [3].\n\n### Cost Comparison\n\nThe enhanced performance of IX resins comes at a premium, with annualized media costs approximately **2-3 times higher** than equivalent GAC treatment capacity [3]. However, IX resins offer longer bed life (12-24 months vs 6-12 months for GAC), partially offsetting the higher media cost through reduced replacement frequency.\n\n### Regeneration Economics\n\nUnlike single-use IX resins, spent GAC can be thermally reactivated, recovering **85-90% of virgin carbon adsorption capacity** while reducing disposal costs by approximately 60% [4]. After three regeneration cycles, thermally reactivated GAC retained 87% of its original capacity, making it economically viable for utilities with access to reactivation facilities.", "vid": "PG_AUDIT_001", "ts": "2026-02-26T10:03:50Z"},

.\outputs\codex_findings\deep_dive_round_7\findings.md:10:  BUG-M-202 is confirmed. `extract_numeric_claims()` only attempts extraction after `_normalize_predicate()` matches one of the hard-coded obesity/cardiometabolic predicate strings. Because the clinical AF anticoagulation corpus discusses endpoints such as stroke, systemic embolism, major bleeding, intracranial hemorrhage, INR/TTR, CHA2DS2-VASc, and HAS-BLED rather than "weight loss" or "hba1c reduction", the detector returns `numeric_claims=0 contradictions=0` before any numeric values are considered. The fix should not replace deterministic numeric comparison with unconstrained LLM judgment. It should add generic numeric-claim mining, then use domain YAML predicate hints for normalization, unit policy, and endpoint aliases.
.\outputs\codex_findings\deep_dive_round_7\findings.md:19:The detector is documented as a general numeric contradiction detector, but its extraction gate is domain-specific. In `src/polaris_graph/retrieval/contradiction_detector.py`, `_EFFICACY_PREDICATES` and `_SAFETY_PREDICATES` contain a narrow clinical-metabolic list: weight loss/body weight, HbA1c/A1c, blood pressure, LDL/cholesterol, MACE/mortality, nausea/vomiting, discontinuation, adverse events, pancreatitis, hypoglycemia, and thyroid C-cell (`:78-93`). `_normalize_predicate()` only returns a predicate if one of those literal substrings appears in the quote (`:134-140`).

.\outputs\codex_findings\deep_dive_round_6\findings.md:105:Tier-balanced selection is only as good as the relevance signal. The first implementation should prefer a simple deterministic lexical scorer plus transparent telemetry over a complex scorer that is hard to test. If later quality data shows lexical ranking is weak, the selector boundary gives a clean place to add a stronger reranker without changing gate semantics again.

.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_4\_stale_v7_misdispatch\codex_stderr.log:310:An adjusted indirect treatment comparison of tirzepatide versus semaglutide 2 mg in patients with type 2 diabetes found that tirzepatide 10 and 15 mg significantly reduced HbA1c versus semaglutide 2 mg, with estimated treatment differences of −0.36% and −0.4%, respectively, and significantly reduced body weight with differences of −3.15 kg and −5.15 kg, respectively.[8] Another indirect comparison in people with obesity or overweight and type 2 diabetes found tirzepatide 10 and 15 mg were associated with significantly greater mean percent weight reductions versus semaglutide 2.4 mg, with mean differences of 2.57% and 4.79%, respectively, and greater HbA1c reductions.[9] In a related obesity trial without diabetes, a direct head-to-head comparison showed a least-squares mean percent change in weight of −20.2% with tirzepatide versus −13.7% with semaglutide.[10] For cardiovascular outcomes in patients with diabetes and cardiovascular disease, tirzepatide was compared with dulaglutide, where a 6-component cardiorenal end point occurred in 23.7% of tirzepatide-treated patients versus 27.4% of dulaglutide-treated patients.[11] One systematic review and meta-analysis of direct comparative studies concluded tirzepatide was significantly superior to semaglutide in achieving weight reduction, with a pooled standardized mean difference of 0.75.[12]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_4\_stale_v7_misdispatch\codex_stderr.log:322:In older patients with type 2 diabetes and diminished β-cell function, a case report suggests tirzepatide may help control postprandial hyperglycemia but may not replace basal insulin for fasting control, requiring monitoring for sarcopenia.[21] In a prespecified analysis of the SUMMIT trial in patients with heart failure with preserved ejection fraction and obesity, tirzepatide's effect on cardiovascular outcomes was consistent regardless of diabetes status, with a hazard ratio of 0.64 (95% CI: 0.35-1.15) for the composite of cardiovascular death or worsening heart failure in patients with type 2 diabetes and 0.61 (95% CI: 0.33-1.10) in those without diabetes (Pinteraction = 0.95).[22] However, weight loss with tirzepatide was less pronounced in patients with type 2 diabetes (10.4% [95% CI: 8.7%-12.2%]) compared to those without diabetes (12.9% [95% CI: 11.2%-14.6%]) (Pinteraction = 0.04).[22] Weight loss was significantly greater in patients with higher baseline body weight (≥75 kg) across all tirzepatide doses.[23] A separate post-hoc analysis of SURPASS trials indicated that tirzepatide was effective in reducing HbA1c and body weight in patients with glutamic acid decarboxylase autoantibodies (GADA-positive), though the HbA1c reduction was slightly less compared to GADA-negative patients (estimated treatment difference 0.21% [95% CI: 0.03, 0.39]; p=0.024).[24]

.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_4\_stale_v7_misdispatch\codex_stderr.log:712:In a direct head-to-head trial in patients with obesity without diabetes, tirzepatide demonstrated superior weight loss compared to semaglutide, with a least-squares mean percent change in weight at week 72 of −20.2% versus −13.7%.[5] Participants were also more likely to achieve weight reductions of at least 10%, 15%, 20%, and 25% with tirzepatide.[5] Another phase 3 trial reported a mean percentage change in body weight with tirzepatide of −20.2% compared with −13.7% for semaglutide at 72 weeks.[6] A real-world retrospective cohort study in patients without diabetes also found greater 6-month mean percentage weight reduction with tirzepatide (−11.15%) versus semaglutide (−8.83%).[7] A systematic review and meta-analysis of direct comparative studies concluded tirzepatide was significantly superior to semaglutide in achieving weight reduction, with a greater standardized mean difference for weight loss.[8] For glycemic control in type 2 diabetes, a network meta-analysis found all tirzepatide doses (5, 10, 15 mg) were comparable to semaglutide 2.0 mg and superior to semaglutide 1.0 mg and 0.5 mg in reducing HbA1c.[9] An indirect treatment comparison also concluded tirzepatide 10 and 15 mg were associated with significantly greater mean percent weight reductions versus semaglutide 2.4 mg in people with obesity and type 2 diabetes.[10] Compared to insulin, tirzepatide showed greater efficacy; a meta-analysis found once-weekly tirzepatide significantly decreased HbA1c and body weight compared with daily insulin glargine and degludec, with a lower risk of hypoglycemia.[11] Specifically, in a trial adding therapy to basal insulin, the mean change in HbA1c at week 52 was −2.1% with tirzepatide versus −1.1% with insulin lispro, with less hypoglycemia and more body weight reduction.[12] Another meta-analysis reported all three tirzepatide doses resulted in a significant reduction in HbA1c and a significant decrease in body weight compared with long-acting insulin.[13] Regarding safety, gastrointestinal events were the most common adverse events for both tirzepatide and semaglutide in the head-to-head obesity trial, with one source reporting at least one adverse event in approximately 76.7% of patients on tirzepatide and 79% on semaglutide.[5][6] A network meta-analysis in patients with type 2 diabetes on basal insulin found tirzepatide showed a statistically significant, higher likelihood of experiencing nausea compared with placebo or exenatide, but no significant difference in treatment discontinuation due to adverse events compared to several GLP-1 receptor agonists.[14]

.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_4\codex_stderr.log:306:Across the SURPASS program, a high proportion of participants achieved A1c targets, with 81% to 97% achieving an A1c <7.0%, 66% to 95% achieving ≤6.5%, and 23% to 62% achieving normoglycemia (A1c <5.7%) across the different doses and trials.[1] In that trial, the proportions of patients achieving an A1c <7.0% were 75.4%, 86.0%, and 84.4% with the three tirzepatide doses, respectively, versus 23.7% with insulin glargine.[2] Tirzepatide also produced significant, dose-dependent weight loss across the type 2 diabetes trials.[1] A systematic review notes that across phase 3 trials, tirzepatide achieved greater reductions in hemoglobin A1c (up to 2.4%) and body weight (up to 22%) compared to GLP-1 receptor agonists.[3] Beyond glycemic and weight control, tirzepatide improved cardiometabolic parameters across the SURPASS trials, including reductions in fasting serum glucose, systolic and diastolic blood pressure, triglycerides, LDL cholesterol, VLDL cholesterol, and increases in HDL cholesterol.[1] An individual participant data meta-analysis of phase 3 trials reported that tirzepatide 15 mg/week significantly reduced the risk of major adverse cardiovascular events compared to comparators, with a hazard ratio of 0.67 (95% CI, 0.51-0.87).[4]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_4\codex_stderr.log:310:The most frequent adverse events associated with tirzepatide are gastrointestinal in nature, with nausea, diarrhea, and vomiting being the most common and typically mild to moderate in severity.[5] In the SURPASS Phase 3 clinical trials for type 2 diabetes, the incidence of gastrointestinal adverse events was dose-dependent, with nausea reported in 20–33% of participants, diarrhea in 18–22%, and vomiting in 8–13%.[5] A systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized clinical trials in patients with diabetes and/or obesity similarly reports that gastrointestinal effects are the most common adverse events.[6] In a retrospective study of adults with type 1 diabetes and obesity, the overall incidence of side effects was 26%, with nausea being the most common at 15%, and only 4% of patients discontinued tirzepatide due to side effects.[7] In the SURMOUNT-1 trial for obesity, the vast majority of participants (90%) who were initially late responders (achieving <5% weight loss at week 12) went on to achieve clinically meaningful weight reduction by week 72, suggesting a generally tolerable profile that allows for continued treatment.[8] Serious adverse events appear uncommon; in a Phase I trial of a bispecific GLP-1/GLP-2 agonist, no serious adverse events or discontinuations due to adverse events occurred, and treatment-emergent adverse events were mostly mild to moderate gastrointestinal events.[9] Furthermore, in the retrospective study of patients with type 1 diabetes, no episodes of severe hypoglycemia or diabetic ketoacidosis were recorded during tirzepatide treatment.[7]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_4\codex_stderr.log:314:Compared with insulin glargine in the SURPASS-4 trial, tirzepatide at 104 weeks led to significantly greater reductions in HbA1c (5 mg: -2.3%, 10 mg: -2.5%, 15 mg: -2.6% vs. -1.0% with insulin glargine) and body weight (5 mg: -7.6 kg, 10 mg: -10.0 kg, 15 mg: -11.4 kg vs. +2.1 kg with insulin glargine).[10] A meta-analysis of six RCTs found tirzepatide reduced body weight more than the GLP-1 receptor agonists semaglutide 1 mg and dulaglutide 1.5 mg, and resulted in a higher proportion of patients achieving weight loss thresholds of ≥5%, ≥10%, and ≥15%.[11] In the head-to-head SURPASS-2 trial, all three tirzepatide doses (5, 10, and 15 mg) demonstrated superior HbA1c and weight reductions compared to once-weekly semaglutide 1 mg at 40 weeks.[12] A model-based simulation of switching from stable GLP-1 RA therapy predicted that transitioning to tirzepatide could yield further HbA1c reductions of 1.95% to 2.46% and body weight reductions of 6.50 to 12.1 kg by week 66.[13] For weight maintenance after initial loss, the SURMOUNT-4 trial in adults with obesity (without diabetes) showed that continuing tirzepatide led to an additional 5.5% weight reduction from weeks 36 to 88, whereas switching to placebo resulted in a 14.0% weight regain over the same period.[14] By week 88, 69.5% of participants continuing tirzepatide achieved ≥20% weight loss from baseline, compared to 12.6% in the placebo group.[14] In a related obesity trial without diabetes, SURMOUNT-1, tirzepatide 15 mg produced a mean weight reduction of 20.9% at 72 weeks versus 3.1% with placebo.[15] Regarding safety, the meta-analysis reported that tirzepatide did not increase the risk of hypoglycemia compared to semaglutide 1 mg.[11] Gastrointestinal adverse events, such as nausea, diarrhea, and vomiting, were more common with tirzepatide than placebo but were predominantly mild to moderate in severity.[14] In SURMOUNT-4, treatment discontinuation due to an adverse event during the double-blind period occurred in 1.8% of the tirzepatide group and 0.9% of the placebo group.[14] A post-hoc analysis of SURMOUNT trials found that adverse events potentially related to macronutrient malnutrition, such as abnormal weight loss or underweight, were uncommon, occurring in 0.12% of tirzepatide-treated participants.[16]

.\outputs\codex_findings\deep_dive_round_4\findings.md:10:  Choose Direction C: keep a deterministic fallback, but make outline degradation explicit with a new manifest status `partial_outline_fallback`, and harden outline validation so prompt contract violations are no longer silently accepted. Real artifacts show collapsed outlines are common enough that aborting immediately would discard reports that can still contain verified prose; however, the current one-section fallback hides under-coverage from downstream readers and contradicts the planner contract. The fix should retry malformed planner output once with a tighter prompt, then either use a deterministic 3-section evidence-balanced fallback when the evidence pool can support it, or abort if even that cannot produce verifiable sections.

.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_9\codex_stderr.log:144:Gastrointestinal adverse events are the most common safety concern with tirzepatide, occurring in a dose-dependent manner.[7] Another systematic review noted that compared to the 5 mg dose, tirzepatide 10 mg and 15 mg were associated with more frequent nausea (P<0.001) and vomiting (P=0.007 for 5 mg vs. 10 mg; P<0.001 for 5 mg vs. 15 mg).[8][9] These events often led to treatment discontinuation; one analysis found drug discontinuation due to adverse events was highest with the 15 mg dose at 10%, and another systematic review reported higher discontinuation rates with tirzepatide 10 mg (pooled RR=1.75) and 15 mg (pooled RR=2.03) compared to GLP-1 receptor agonists.[7][8][9] In the obesity trial SURMOUNT-4, during the initial open-label lead-in period with tirzepatide, 35.5% of participants experienced nausea, 21.1% diarrhea, 20.7% constipation, and 16.3% vomiting.[10][11] In SURPASS-AP-Combo, where tirzepatide was compared to insulin glargine, no severe hypoglycemia was reported.[6] A meta-analysis found the incidence of mild hypoglycemia (blood glucose <70 mg/dL) was highest with the tirzepatide 10 mg dose at 22.6% (95% CI, 9.2%-39.8%).[7] However, another systematic review concluded the overall safety profile was similar to GLP-1 receptor agonists except for a higher risk of hypoglycemia with the 15 mg dose (pooled RR=3.83, 95% CI 1.19-12.30) and higher discontinuation rates.[8][9] A meta-analysis found rates of MTC and other serious events like acute pancreatitis and cholecystitis were ≤1% across all tirzepatide doses.[7] In the SURMOUNT-CN obesity trial, one case of papillary thyroid cancer occurred in a participant with a history of thyroid nodules receiving tirzepatide 15 mg, which was considered unrelated to treatment by the investigator, and no cases of medullary thyroid cancer were reported.[12] Similarly, in the SURMOUNT-4 trial, no cases of medullary thyroid carcinoma were reported.[10][11] A real-world pharmacovigilance analysis identified a signal for medullary thyroid carcinoma with an EB05 of 3.55, based on 3 reported cases.[13] Other adverse events of interest include pancreatitis, with the same pharmacovigilance analysis reporting 190 cases and a signal strength (EB05=6.85), though adjudicated confirmed cases in trials were low; for example, SURMOUNT-4 reported no adjudication-confirmed cases of pancreatitis.[13][10][11] Gallbladder-related events such as cholelithiasis and acute cholecystitis have also been reported at low frequencies.[10][11][12][7]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_9\codex_stderr.log:148:A systematic review and network meta-analysis of subcutaneous GLP-1 receptor agonists and tirzepatide concluded that all three tirzepatide doses were comparable to semaglutide 2.0 mg and superior to semaglutide 1.0 mg and 0.5 mg in reducing HbA1c, while demonstrating greater weight loss than the corresponding semaglutide doses.[14] A post hoc analysis of SURPASS-2 found tirzepatide increased the odds of achieving composite therapeutic targets, such as HbA1c <7.0% with weight loss >10%, compared to semaglutide 1 mg.[15] For weight loss specifically in populations with overweight or obesity, a meta-analysis of direct comparative studies reported a standardized mean difference of 0.75 favoring tirzepatide over semaglutide, and a real-world evidence study in a US cohort with overweight or obesity reported patients on tirzepatide had significantly higher rates of achieving ≥5%, ≥10%, and ≥15% weight loss within one year.[16][17] In SURPASS-3 versus insulin degludec, tirzepatide 5 mg, 10 mg, and 15 mg produced greater HbA1c reductions and body weight reductions ranging from -7.5 kg to -12.9 kg, whereas insulin degludec was associated with weight gain of +2.3 kg.[3] Similarly, in SURPASS-4 versus insulin glargine in patients at increased cardiovascular risk, all tirzepatide doses provided superior HbA1c reductions and weight reductions between -6.4 kg and -10.6 kg, compared to weight gain of +1.9 kg with insulin glargine.[5] Another systematic review and meta-analysis confirmed these findings, reporting tirzepatide was associated with significantly greater proportions of patients achieving HbA1c targets of <7.0% (80-89% vs. 49%) and weight loss ≥5% (62-83% vs. 7%) compared to long-acting insulin.[18] A network meta-analysis in patients on basal insulin background therapy found tirzepatide 5, 10, and 15 mg provided statistically significant greater reductions in HbA1c and body weight versus GLP-1 receptor agonists dulaglutide, exenatide, and lixisenatide.[19] The indirect comparison versus semaglutide 2 mg reported a similar overall safety profile, though tirzepatide 10 mg and 15 mg had higher odds of treatment discontinuation due to adverse events.[20] Compared to basal insulin, tirzepatide is associated with more gastrointestinal adverse events but a significantly lower risk of hypoglycemia.[21][18]

.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_9\codex_stderr.log:3056:Hedging grammar: ChatGPT is the best hedger. .\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_9\codex_stderr.log:3070:Claim architecture: Gemini often uses: clinical rationale -> named trial -> design/N/baseline -> endpoint/result -> interpretive flourish. The architecture is richer than V17, though less statistically disciplined than ChatGPT because CIs and p-values are often absent in narrative claims.

.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_8\codex_stderr.log:472:A post hoc analysis of SURPASS-2 reported that a higher proportion of participants achieved multiple therapeutic targets (e.g., HbA1c <7.0% and weight loss >10%) with tirzepatide compared to semaglutide, with 57% of those on tirzepatide 15 mg meeting three or more standard targets versus 34% on semaglutide.[13] A systematic review and meta-analysis of direct comparative studies in overweight and obese adults, including populations with and without type 2 diabetes, found tirzepatide was significantly superior to semaglutide for weight reduction (SMD 0.75) and had higher odds of achieving ≥10% weight loss (OR 0.21).[14] A network meta-analysis in patients on basal insulin background therapy found tirzepatide 5, 10, and 15 mg provided statistically significant, greater reductions in HbA1c and body weight versus GLP-1 RA comparators including dulaglutide, exenatide, and lixisenatide.[15] The cardiovascular safety of tirzepatide versus dulaglutide was established in the SURPASS-CVOT trial, which met noninferiority for major adverse cardiovascular events (MACE) with a hazard ratio of 0.92 (95.3% CI 0.83 to 1.01).[16]

.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_7\codex_stderr.log:1392:In the pivotal SURPASS-1 trial, tirzepatide monotherapy at 5, 10, and 15 mg doses reduced HbA1c by −1.87%, −1.89%, and −2.07% from baseline, respectively, compared to a +0.04% change with placebo over 40 weeks.[1] When compared to basal insulin in SURPASS-3, tirzepatide 5, 10, and 15 mg produced HbA1c reductions of −1.93%, −2.20%, and −2.37%, respectively, versus −1.34% with insulin degludec over 52 weeks.[1][2] In the higher cardiovascular risk population of SURPASS-4, tirzepatide doses led to reductions of −2.24%, −2.43%, and −2.58% versus −1.44% with insulin glargine.[1][3] In the Asia-Pacific SURPASS-AP-Combo trial, tirzepatide was superior to insulin glargine, with HbA1c reductions of −2.24%, −2.44%, and −2.49% for the 5, 10, and 15 mg doses, respectively, compared to −0.95%.[4] A post-hoc analysis of SURPASS-AP-Combo found that patients with a higher baseline HbA1c (>8.5%) experienced significantly greater HbA1c reductions with all tirzepatide doses compared to those with lower baseline HbA1c.[5] Tirzepatide also demonstrated rapid glycemic control, with a median time to achieve HbA1c <7.0% of 8.1 weeks across doses, which was faster than the 12.0 weeks for semaglutide 1 mg in SURPASS-2 and 12.1 weeks for insulin degludec in SURPASS-3.[6] For weight loss in type 2 diabetes, tirzepatide induced substantial reductions across the SURPASS program.[1] In SURPASS-1, body weight changes were −7.0 kg, −7.8 kg, and −9.5 kg for the 5, 10, and 15 mg doses, respectively, versus −0.7 kg with placebo.[1] In SURPASS-2, weight loss was −7.8 kg, −10.3 kg, and −12.4 kg with tirzepatide compared to −6.2 kg with semaglutide 1 mg.[1][7] In SURPASS-3, weight reductions were −7.5 kg, −10.7 kg, and −12.9 kg versus a +2.3 kg gain with insulin degludec.[1][2] In SURPASS-4, weight changes were −7.1 kg, −9.5 kg, and −11.7 kg with tirzepatide versus a +1.9 kg gain with insulin glargine.[1][3] A post-hoc analysis of SURPASS-AP-Combo showed that patients with higher baseline body weight (≥75 kg) experienced significantly greater absolute weight loss across all tirzepatide doses.[5] In SURPASS-2, the median time to first achieve ≥5% weight loss was faster with tirzepatide 5 mg (16.0 weeks) and 10/15 mg (12.4 weeks) than with semaglutide 1 mg (24.0 weeks).[6] A post-hoc analysis of SURMOUNT-2, a trial in patients with type 2 diabetes and obesity, found that the magnitude of placebo-corrected weight loss with tirzepatide 15 mg varied inversely with baseline HbA1c, with the greatest reduction (−17.7%) seen in the subgroup with HbA1c <7%.[8] In obesity trials without diabetes, weight loss was even more pronounced.[1] In SURMOUNT-1, the maximum tolerated dose of tirzepatide led to a mean weight reduction of −20.9% at 72 weeks versus −3.1% with placebo.[9]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_7\codex_stderr.log:1400:This trial also found tirzepatide superior for weight loss, with significantly greater reductions in body weight versus semaglutide 1 mg and higher proportions of patients achieving weight loss targets of ≥5%, ≥10%, and ≥15%.[7] Another aITC found all tirzepatide doses provided statistically significantly greater reductions in HbA1c and body weight compared to semaglutide 0.5 mg.[17] A post-hoc analysis of SURPASS-2 reported tirzepatide increased the odds of achieving stringent composite therapeutic targets (e.g., HbA1c <7% with weight loss >10%) compared to semaglutide 1 mg.[18] A systematic review and meta-analysis of direct comparative studies concluded tirzepatide was significantly superior to semaglutide for weight reduction, with a pooled standardized mean difference of 0.75 (95% CI: 0.52 to 0.92).[19] Model-based simulations suggest switching from stable maintenance doses of semaglutide or dulaglutide to tirzepatide can lead to further improvements in HbA1c and body weight.[20] A network meta-analysis in patients on basal insulin found tirzepatide 5, 10, and 15 mg provided statistically significant, greater reductions in HbA1c and body weight versus GLP-1 RA comparators dulaglutide, exenatide, and lixisenatide.[21] The incidence of hypoglycemia (blood glucose <54 mg/dL) was low and comparable between groups.[7] An aITC reported comparable adverse event profiles between tirzepatide and semaglutide 0.5 mg, with no statistically significant differences in the odds of gastrointestinal AEs for any tirzepatide dose.[17] The network meta-analysis against other GLP-1 RAs found tirzepatide had a statistically significant higher likelihood of nausea versus placebo and exenatide 2 mg, but no significant difference versus dulaglutide, and no significant differences in treatment discontinuation due to adverse events.[21]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_7\codex_stderr.log:1408:In older adults with type 2 diabetes, a post-hoc analysis of the pooled SURPASS-1 to -5 trials found that participants aged ≥65 years with a body mass index <30 kg/m² treated with tirzepatide experienced clinically meaningful HbA1c reductions of −1.97% to −2.10% across doses.[25] Another post-hoc analysis of all five SURPASS studies confirmed that reductions in HbA1c at week 40 or 52 were consistent for prespecified age subgroups (<65 and ≥65 years) compared with placebo or active comparators.[26] In that same trial, tirzepatide produced less weight loss at 52 weeks in patients with type 2 diabetes (−10.4%) compared to those without diabetes (−12.9%), though reductions in paracardiac fat and left ventricular mass were similar between groups.[27] For Asian patients, the phase 3 SURPASS-AP-Combo trial, conducted at 66 hospitals in China, South Korea, Australia, and India, demonstrated the efficacy of tirzepatide versus insulin glargine in a predominantly Chinese population (83.2% from China).[4] In that trial, all tirzepatide doses were superior to insulin glargine for HbA1c reduction at week 40, with least squares mean reductions of −2.24% (5 mg), −2.44% (10 mg), and −2.49% (15 mg) compared to −0.95% with glargine, and led to body weight reductions of −5.0 kg, −7.0 kg, and −7.2 kg, respectively, versus a gain of 1.5 kg with glargine.[4] A post-hoc subgroup analysis of SURPASS-AP-Combo found that reductions in HbA1c and body weight with tirzepatide were generally consistent across subgroups defined by age, sex, baseline HbA1c, BMI, body weight, diabetes duration, and concomitant oral medications, though a significantly greater HbA1c reduction was seen in younger patients (<65 years) with the 15 mg dose and in those with higher baseline body weight (≥75 kg) with the 10 mg dose.[5] In Japanese patients, the SURPASS J-mono trial demonstrated the efficacy and safety of tirzepatide monotherapy compared to dulaglutide, while the SURPASS J-combo trial evaluated tirzepatide as an add-on to a single oral antihyperglycemic medication.[11][28] Regarding cardiovascular risk, the SURPASS-CVOT trial in patients with type 2 diabetes and atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease found the primary composite endpoint of cardiovascular death, myocardial infarction, or stroke occurred in 12.2% of the tirzepatide group versus 13.1% of the dulaglutide group (hazard ratio 0.92), meeting non-inferiority.[29] Furthermore, in the SURPASS-SWITCH trial, which evaluated switching from dulaglutide to tirzepatide, the treatment effect for HbA1c and body weight reduction was consistent across baseline subgroups including age, sex, BMI, and HbA1c, with no significant treatment-by-subgroup interactions for most factors.[30]

.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_10\codex_stderr.log:227:outputs/full_scale_v21/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/report.md:5:In the SURPASS-1 trial of patients with type 2 diabetes (T2D) inadequately controlled by diet and exercise alone, tirzepatide at 5, 10, and 15 mg doses reduced HbA1c by −1.87%, −1.89%, and −2.07% from baseline versus a 0.04% increase with placebo at 40 weeks.[1][2] Tirzepatide also demonstrated superior efficacy versus basal insulins; in SURPASS-3, it reduced HbA1c by −1.93% to −2.37% across doses versus −1.34% with insulin degludec over 52 weeks, and in SURPASS-4, it reduced HbA1c by −2.24% to −2.58% versus −1.44% with insulin glargine.[1][3][4] For patients on basal insulin with inadequate control, adding tirzepatide in SURPASS-6 was more effective than adding prandial insulin lispro, reducing HbA1c by −2.1% versus −1.1% and reducing body weight by −6.7 to −11.0 kg versus a 3.2 kg gain.[1][5] A post-hoc analysis of SURPASS-2 found tirzepatide was superior to semaglutide 1 mg in achieving composite therapeutic targets, with 57% of patients on tirzepatide 15 mg meeting three or more standard targets (HbA1c <7.0%, weight loss >5%, systolic blood pressure <130 mmHg) versus 34% on semaglutide.[6] Efficacy was consistent across key subgroups; in SURPASS-AP-Combo, greater HbA1c reductions were seen in patients with higher baseline HbA1c (>8.5%), and greater weight loss was seen in those with higher baseline body weight (≥75 kg) across all tirzepatide doses.[7] A pre-planned analysis showed tirzepatide enabled faster achievement of glycemic and weight loss thresholds than semaglutide 1 mg or insulin degludec, with a median time to reach HbA1c <7.0% of 8.1 weeks versus 12.0 and 12.1 weeks, respectively.[8]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_10\codex_stderr.log:231:outputs/full_scale_v21/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/report.md:13:A systematic review and network meta-analysis of subcutaneous GLP-1 receptor agonists and tirzepatide found that all tirzepatide doses were comparable to semaglutide 2.0 mg and superior to semaglutide 1.0 mg and 0.5 mg in reducing HbA1c, with tirzepatide 15 mg ranked as the most efficacious treatment.[17] Tirzepatide also enabled more patients to achieve stringent glycemic targets faster than semaglutide 1 mg, with a median time to achieve HbA1c <7.0% of 8.1 weeks versus 12.0 weeks and to achieve HbA1c ≤6.5% of 12.1 weeks versus 15.7 weeks.[8] A meta-analysis of direct comparative studies in overweight and obese adults, which included populations with and without type 2 diabetes, found tirzepatide was superior to semaglutide for weight reduction, with a standardized mean difference of 0.75 (95% CI 0.52 to 0.92) and greater odds of achieving ≥10% weight loss (OR 0.21, 95% CI 0.06 to 0.78).[18] A post-hoc analysis of SURPASS-2 reported that tirzepatide increased the odds of achieving an intensive weight loss target of >15% compared to semaglutide (OR 3.86, 95% CI 2.69 to 5.55).[6] In SURPASS-3 versus insulin degludec, tirzepatide 5, 10, and 15 mg produced greater HbA1c reductions and significant weight loss, while insulin degludec was associated with weight gain.[3] Furthermore, a network meta-analysis in patients on basal insulin background therapy found tirzepatide 5, 10, and 15 mg provided statistically significant greater reductions in HbA1c and body weight compared to the GLP-1 RAs dulaglutide, exenatide, and lixisenatide.[19] The incidence of hypoglycemia (blood glucose <54 mg/dL) was low and similar between tirzepatide and semaglutide 1 mg.[20] The network meta-analysis comparing subcutaneous agents found both tirzepatide and semaglutide increased the incidence of gastrointestinal adverse events compared to placebo, with no increased risk of serious adverse events or severe hypoglycemia for either.[17]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_10\codex_stderr.log:233:outputs/full_scale_v21/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/report.md:17:Tirzepatide demonstrates a clear dose-dependent effect on both glycemic control and weight reduction in patients with type 2 diabetes.[1][21][22] Another meta-analysis concluded that within the 5–15 mg range, the effect on lowering glycemia and reducing weight is dose-dependent, with tirzepatide 15 mg achieving the best efficacy.[21] The time to achieve glycemic and weight loss targets is also dose-responsive and faster with higher tirzepatide doses compared to active comparators.[8] A pre-planned analysis of SURPASS-2 and SURPASS-3 found the median time to first achieve HbA1c <7.0% was 8.1 weeks for all tirzepatide doses, compared to 12.0 weeks for semaglutide 1 mg and 12.1 weeks for insulin degludec.[8] Furthermore, the median time to achieve ≥5% weight loss in SURPASS-2 was 16.0 weeks with tirzepatide 5 mg and 12.4 weeks with the 10 mg and 15 mg doses, compared to 24.0 weeks with semaglutide 1 mg.[8] Subgroup analyses indicate that the efficacy of tirzepatide is generally consistent across different patient demographics, though some variations exist.[7] In the SURPASS-AP-Combo trial, patients with a higher baseline HbA1c (>8.5%) experienced significantly greater HbA1c reductions with all tirzepatide doses, while patients with a higher baseline body weight (≥75 kg) experienced significantly greater weight reductions across all doses.[7] An indirect treatment comparison estimated that tirzepatide 10 mg and 15 mg provided significantly greater HbA1c and body weight reductions compared to semaglutide 2.4 mg (an obesity dose in a related population), while tirzepatide 5 mg showed similar efficacy to semaglutide 2.4 mg.[23]

.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_10\codex_stderr.log:386:   5: In the SURPASS-1 trial of patients with type 2 diabetes (T2D) inadequately controlled by diet and exercise alone, tirzepatide at 5, 10, and 15 mg doses reduced HbA1c by −1.87%, −1.89%, and −2.07% from baseline versus a 0.04% increase with placebo at 40 weeks.[1][2] Tirzepatide also demonstrated superior efficacy versus basal insulins; in SURPASS-3, it reduced HbA1c by −1.93% to −2.37% across doses versus −1.34% with insulin degludec over 52 weeks, and in SURPASS-4, it reduced HbA1c by −2.24% to −2.58% versus −1.44% with insulin glargine.[1][3][4] For patients on basal insulin with inadequate control, adding tirzepatide in SURPASS-6 was more effective than adding prandial insulin lispro, reducing HbA1c by −2.1% versus −1.1% and reducing body weight by −6.7 to −11.0 kg versus a 3.2 kg gain.[1][5] A post-hoc analysis of SURPASS-2 found tirzepatide was superior to semaglutide 1 mg in achieving composite therapeutic targets, with 57% of patients on tirzepatide 15 mg meeting three or more standard targets (HbA1c <7.0%, weight loss >5%, systolic blood pressure <130 mmHg) versus 34% on semaglutide.[6] Efficacy was consistent across key subgroups; in SURPASS-AP-Combo, greater HbA1c reductions were seen in patients with higher baseline HbA1c (>8.5%), and greater weight loss was seen in those with higher baseline body weight (≥75 kg) across all tirzepatide doses.[7] A pre-planned analysis showed tirzepatide enabled faster achievement of glycemic and weight loss thresholds than semaglutide 1 mg or insulin degludec, with a median time to reach HbA1c <7.0% of 8.1 weeks versus 12.0 and 12.1 weeks, respectively.[8]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_10\codex_stderr.log:394:  13: A systematic review and network meta-analysis of subcutaneous GLP-1 receptor agonists and tirzepatide found that all tirzepatide ChatGPT is stronger: it separates direct versus indirect comparator evidence, labels semaglutide 2 mg comparisons as inferential, and correctly distinguishes SURPASS-CVOT noninferiority from superiority (compare_chatgpt_dr.txt:63-66,701-762,952-959,1061-1085). .\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_6\codex_stderr.log:284:    "url": "https://dom-pubs.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/dom.14775"
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_6\codex_stderr.log:347:    "url": "https://assets.cureus.com/uploads/review_article/pdf/184390/20230829-25847-16h6sdo.pdf"
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_6\codex_stderr.log:536:Gastrointestinal adverse events are the most common tolerability concern with tirzepatide and are typically mild to moderate in severity.[6] A systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized clinical trials reported that gastrointestinal adverse events were the most frequently reported category in tirzepatide-related individual case safety reports in a real-world pharmacovigilance database, with pancreatitis and vomiting being the most common specific events.[7] For vomiting, incidence with tirzepatide 15 mg ranged from 6% to 18.1%, and for diarrhea, it ranged from 12% to 31%.[1] One systematic review found that compared to 5 mg, tirzepatide 10 mg and 15 mg were associated with more frequent nausea, and 15 mg led to more diarrhea than 5 mg, suggesting some dose-dependence for specific gastrointestinal events.[8] The risk of hypoglycemia with tirzepatide is generally low when not used with insulin or insulin secretagogues.[9] Regarding thyroid safety, real-world pharmacovigilance data has detected a disproportionate reporting signal for medullary thyroid cancer with tirzepatide (ROR 13.67, 95% CI 4.35–42.96).[10] This aligns with a boxed warning for thyroid C-cell tumors seen in rodents, and it is unknown whether tirzepatide causes such tumors in humans, including medullary thyroid carcinoma.[11] However, the same real-world analysis noted tirzepatide exhibited a similar risk of medullary thyroid cancer compared to GLP-1 receptor agonists.[10] Furthermore, a review noted that pivotal trials in the SURPASS program did not report clinically meaningful increases in autoimmune thyroid disease or sustained thyroid dysfunction.[12] Discontinuation due to adverse events appears dose-related; one systematic review found the risk of discontinuation by adverse event was significantly higher with tirzepatide 10 mg (pooled RR=1.75, 95%CI[1.16-2.63]) and 15 mg (pooled RR=2.03, 95%CI [1.37-3.01]) compared to GLP-1 receptor agonists.[8]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_6\codex_stderr.log:544:This dose-response relationship for HbA1c reduction was consistent across trials; in SURPASS-4 at 104 weeks, mean reductions were -2.3%, -2.5%, and -2.6% for the 5 mg, 10 mg, and 15 mg doses, respectively.[3] Weight loss also followed a clear dose-dependent pattern, with greater reductions at higher doses.[20] In SURPASS-2, body weight reductions were -7.8 kg, -10.3 kg, and -12.4 kg for tirzepatide 5 mg, 10 mg, and 15 mg, respectively, a trend replicated in SURPASS-4 with reductions of -7.6 kg, -10.0 kg, and -11.4 kg at 104 weeks.[21][3] A meta-analysis confirmed that compared to the 5 mg dose, the 10 mg and 15 mg doses provided significantly greater weight loss of -2.64 kg and -4.26 kg, respectively.[22] The proportion of patients achieving weight loss thresholds also increased with dose; in SURPASS-2, 65%, 76%, and 80% of patients achieved ≥5% weight loss on 5 mg, 10 mg, and 15 mg, respectively, while ≥15% weight loss was achieved by 15%, 24%, and 36%.[1] An adjusted indirect treatment comparison estimated that tirzepatide 10 mg and 15 mg provided significantly greater HbA1c and body weight reductions compared to semaglutide 2 mg, while the 5 mg dose showed similar efficacy to semaglutide 2 mg.[13] The time to achieve glycemic and weight loss targets was faster with all tirzepatide doses compared to active controls, with the median time to reach HbA1c <7.0% being 8.1 weeks for each tirzepatide dose versus 12.0 weeks for semaglutide 1 mg.[2] Regarding safety, gastrointestinal adverse events were common and showed a dose-dependent increase in incidence.[20] A meta-analysis reported that the risk of total adverse events was significantly higher with the 10 mg and 15 mg doses compared to the 5 mg dose.[22] One meta-analysis concluded that within the 5–15 mg range, glycemic and weight efficacy is dose-dependent, with the 15 mg dose providing the greatest efficacy benefit, while the 5 mg dose may offer a more favorable safety profile.[20]
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_6\codex_stderr.log:548:A post-hoc subgroup analysis of SURPASS-AP-Combo found that the efficacy of tirzepatide was generally consistent across subgroups defined by age, sex, baseline HbA1c, BMI, body weight, and diabetes duration, though some differences were observed.[5] Patients with higher baseline body weight (≥75 kg) experienced significantly greater weight loss across all tirzepatide doses compared to those weighing <75 kg.[5] A post-hoc analysis of pooled SURPASS-1 to -5 data reported that tirzepatide's effect on HbA1c reduction was consistent between participants aged <65 and ≥65 years.[23] In a small observational pilot study of older Japanese patients (mean age 79.5 years), tirzepatide initiation improved time in range from 53.2% to 78.9% as measured by continuous glucose monitoring.[24] For patients with high cardiovascular risk, the SURPASS-4 trial specifically enrolled individuals with type 2 diabetes and increased cardiovascular risk, demonstrating superior HbA1c and weight reductions with tirzepatide versus insulin glargine.[25] The subsequent SURPASS-CVOT trial, which enrolled patients with type 2 diabetes and established atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease, found tirzepatide was non-inferior to dulaglutide for the primary composite cardiovascular endpoint (hazard ratio 0.92; 95% CI 0.83 to 1.01).[18] A real-world trial emulation analysis benchmarking against SURPASS-CVOT estimated a hazard ratio of 0.87 (95% CI 0.75 to 1.01) for a composite outcome including mortality for tirzepatide versus dulaglutide in an expanded population.[19] The safety profile, characterized predominantly by gastrointestinal adverse events, was generally consistent across these populations and trials.[4][26][5][18]

.\outputs\codex_findings\m44_code_audit_pass2\findings.md:9:1. `src/polaris_graph/generator/multi_section_generator.py:2363` - The regen replacement logic never sees the first-pass violation count, so `len(new_viols) < orig_viols_count` is effectively dead. `m44_validator_violations` is initialized empty at line 2281, first-pass violations are used only to append section indexes at lines 2290-2296, and the list is not populated until the final validator pass at line 2379. As a result, `orig_viols_count` is always 0 during regen comparison, and a regen with fewer-but-nonzero violations is rejected even though the pass-2 contract says to replace when it has fewer violations. .\outputs\codex_findings\m9_review\findings.md:8:   The phase matcher is not aligned to the real V30 sweep output. The checked-in phase-2 runs, including run-14, emit `[scope]`, `[retrieval]`, `[adequacy]`, `[generation]`, `[judge]`, `[eval_gate]`, `[V30-P2]`, `[V30]`, and `[status]`, not the canonical strings used here (`scripts/run_honest_sweep_r3.py:466-468,592,666-681,1052-1053,1094-1100,1201-1202,1693-1714,1750,1896-1923`; `outputs/full_scale_v30_phase2_run14/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/run_log.txt:6,9,12,17-20,27,33-40,42`). Running the current classifier over run-14 only yields `generation_started` and `v30_phase1`, so a live job will effectively jump `1 -> 70 -> 90 -> completed`. Also, the current milestone order is not the script’s real order: `[V30-P2]` logs happen before evaluator/judge, while the `M-56` / `[V30]` lines happen after `eval_gate`. If you only add missing patterns without reordering milestones, progress will regress.

.\web\public\canonical_bundles\v1_canonical_success\sources\ev_015.txt:1:Treatment with tirzepatide led to significant weight loss in adults with obesity or overweight with weight-related comorbidities (excluding type 2 diabetes), according to results from the phase 3 SURMOUNT-3 and SURMOUNT-4 studies. Tirzepatide is an investigational once-weekly, dual glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide and glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonist that integrates the actions of both incretins into a single molecule. The multicenter, randomized, double-blind, parallel, placebo-controlled SURMOUNT-3 trial (ClinicalTrials.gov Identifier: [NCT04657016](https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT04657016?term=NCT04657016&rank=1)) evaluated the efficacy and safety of tirzepatide for 72 weeks after a 12-week intensive lifestyle intervention lead-in period in adults with obesity or overweight with weight-related comorbidities, excluding type 2 diabetes. At the end of the 12-week period, study participants (N=579) achieved 6.9% mean weight loss and were randomly assigned 1:1 to receive tirzepatide or placebo subcutaneously once weekly. The efficacy estimand (efficacy prior to discontinuation of study drug) results demonstrated that treatment with tirzepatide was associated with an additional 21.1% reduction in body weight over 72 weeks vs a 3.3% weight regain for the placebo group. Over the 72 weeks, 94.4% of patients in the tirzepatide arm achieved at least a 5% body weight reduction from randomization vs 10.7% of those receiving placebo. The total mean body weight re
.\web\public\canonical_bundles\v1_canonical_success\sources\ev_015.txt:5:ide for 72 weeks after a 12-week intensive lifestyle intervention lead-in period in adults with obesity or overweight with weight-related comorbidities, excluding type 2 diabetes. At the end of the 12-week period, study participants (N=579) achieved 6.9% mean weight loss and were randomly assigned 1:1 to receive tirzepatide or placebo subcutaneously once weekly. The efficacy estimand (efficacy prior to discontinuation of study drug) results demonstrated that treatment with tirzepatide was associated with an additional 21.1% reduction in body weight over 72 weeks vs a 3.3% weight regain for the placebo group. Over the 72 weeks, 94.4% of patients in the tirzepatide arm achieved at least a 5% body weight reduction from randomization vs 10.7% of those receiving placebo. The total mean body weight reduction for patients treated with tirzepatide from study entry after 12 weeks of lifestyle intervention to 72 weeks of treatment was 26.6%. The treatment-regimen estimand (estimated average treatment effect regardless of treatment discontinuation) results demonstrated that tirzepatide was associated with an additional 18.4% reduction in body weight over 72 weeks vs a 2.5% regain with placebo. Moreover, 87.5% of patients in the tirzepatide arm achieved at least a 5% body weight reduction from randomization vs 16.5% of those in the placebo arm. The total mean body weight reduction for patients treated with tirzepatide from study entry after 12 weeks of lifestyle intervention to 72 weeks of treatment was 24.3%. The multicenter, randomized, double-blind, parallel, placebo-controlled SURMOUNT-4 trial (ClinicalTrials.gov Identifier: [NCT04660643](https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT04660643?term=NCT04660643&rank=1)) evaluated the efficacy and safety of tirz

One immediate event was reported in the tirzepatide 15-mg group. ¶ Diabetic retinopathy was confirmed by funduscopic examination. Glycemic End Points At 40 weeks, reductions in the mean glycated hemoglobin level with tirzepatide at a dose of 5 mg, 10 mg, and 15 mg were −2.01 percentage points, −2.24 percentage points, and −2.30 percentage points, respectively, as compared with −1.86 percentage points with semaglutide (Figure 1A). All tirzepatide doses were found to be superior to semaglutide, with estimated treatment differences of −0.15 percentage points (95% confidence interval [CI], −0.28 to −0.03; P=0.02) with tirzepatide 5 mg, −0.39 percentage points (95% CI, −0.51 to −0.26; P<0.001) with tirzepatide 10 mg, and −0.45 percentage points (95% CI, −0.57 to −0.32; P<0.001) with tirzepatide 15 mg. The glycated hemoglobin level over time is shown in (Figure 1B). A total of 82 to 86% of the patients who received tirzepatide and 79% of those who received semaglutide had a decrease in the glycated hemoglobin level to less than 7.0%. A total of 69 to 80% of the patients who received tirzepatide and 64% of those who received semaglutide had a decrease in the glycated hemoglobin level to 6.5% or less, and 27 to 46% and 19%, respectively, had a decrease in the glycated hemoglobin level to less than 5.7% (Fig. 1C). A subgroup analysis involving patients with a glycated hemoglobin level above 8.5% showed mean reductions in the glycated hemoglobin level of −3.22 percentage points with tirzepatide at a dose of 15 mg as compared with −2.68 percentage points with semaglutide (Fig. S8). Figure 1 With the use of the efficacy estimand at 40 weeks, greater reductions in the fasting serum glucose level were observed with all tirzepatide doses than with semaglutide (Figure 1D and Fig. S9). .\web\public\canonical_bundles\v1_canonical_success\sources\ev_002.txt:29:diagnosed according to the World Health Organization (1999) or American Diabetes Association (1997) criteria. Reviews, letters, case reports, nonhuman studies, editorials, commentaries, expert opinions, non-RCTs, and meta-analyses were excluded. ### 2.4 Outcome measures of efficacy and safety The change in HbA1c from the baseline was considered the primary outcome of efficacy. The secondary endpoints included proportions of patients with HbA1c 5% of usual body weight (American College of Cardiology/[American Heart Association Task Force on Practice Guidelines, 2014](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B1)). Our meta-analysis results demonstrated that loss of body weight in the tirzepatide group was from 7.25 kg to 10.36 kg, whether used as monotherapy (7.40 kg) or add-on therapy (8.11 kg). Tirzepatide therapy led to a greater target of the percentage of patients who reached the body weight reduction of ≥5%, ≥10%, and ≥15%, whether used as monotherapy or add-on therapy. More interestingly, weight loss was observed at all doses of tirzepatide within 4 weeks after the start of treatment, and this continued until week 40 or 52; no dose of tirzepatide reached a plateau ([Ludvik et al., 2021](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B23); [Rosenstock et al., 2021](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B30)). The effect of tirzepatide on weight loss is expected. The subpopulation of the SURPASS-3 study showed that tirzepatide led to a significant reduction in liver fat content, volume of visceral adipose tissue, and abdominal subcutaneous adipose tissue volumes compared with insulin degludec ([Gastaldelli et al., 2022](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B14)). GLP-1 RAs not only show a good hypoglycemic effect but also demonstrate the efficacy of weight loss. A meta-analysis evaluated the weight reduction effects of GLP-1 RAs and exhibited that, compared to placebo, GLP-1 RAs led to significant body weight reduction ([Htike et al., 2017](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B19)). As for semaglutide administered in patients with T2D, compared with placebo, subcutaneous semaglutide led to body weight loss (WMD: -2.73 kg and -4.09 kg, for 0.5 mg and 1 mg, respectively). Oral administration showed similar effects ([Zhong et al., 2021](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B38)). For treatment with dulaglutide in patients with T2D, the mean weight loss was 0.73 kg in the 0.75 mg dulaglutide group and 1.27 kg in the 1.5 mg dulaglutide group ([Qie et al., 2020](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/pharmacology/articles/10.3389/fphar.2022.1016639/full#B29)). Our meta-analysis included semaglutide 1 mg and dulaglutide 1.5 mg as the control and presented that tirzepatide reduced body weight greater than the two GLP-1 RAs and resulted in a higher proportion of body weight reaching ≥5%, ≥10%, and ≥15%. .\web\public\canonical_bundles\v1_canonical_success\evidence_pool.json:7:      "full_text": "Abstract This article reviews the efficacy and safety data of tirzepatide, a once-weekly, novel glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide and glucagon-like peptide 1 (GLP-1) receptor agonist approved in the United States, the European Union, and other regions for the treatment of type 2 diabetes. All doses of tirzepatide demonstrated superiority in reducing A1C and body weight from baseline versus placebo or active comparators. The safety profile of tirzepatide was consistent with that of the GLP-1 receptor agonist class, with mild to moderate and transient gastrointestinal side effects being the most common adverse events. With clinically and statistically significant reductions in A1C and body weight without increased risk of hypoglycemia in various populations, tirzepatide has demonstrated potential as a first-in-class treatment option for many people with type 2 diabetes. Type 2 diabetes is a metabolic condition caused by increased insulin resistance and progressive \u03b2-cell failure in the pancreas, resulting in decreased insulin secretion and hyperglycemia. Chronic hyperglycemia can ultimately lead to both micro- and macrovascular complications, which can be prevented with appropriate glycemic control. Obesity is a major factor in the pathophysiology of type 2 diabetes; therefore, treatment of type 2 diabetes should address hyperglycemia and comorbidities ([1](#B1),[2](#B2)). Glucagon-like peptide 1 (GLP-1) receptor agonists are commonly used as first injectable therapy\n\n[...]\n\nrther studies are needed to evaluate these potential mechanisms of action in humans. A phase 2 study assessed the efficacy and safety of four doses (1, 5, 10, and 15 mg) of tirzepatide versus placebo and an active comparator (once-weekly dulaglutide 1.5 mg) in patients with type 2 diabetes ([20](#B20)). The study demonstrated the superiority of tirzepatide 5, 10, and 15 mg versus placebo and dulaglutide 1.5 mg in decreasing A1C at week 26. Likewise, tirzepatide 5, 10, and 15 mg were superior to placebo in body weight reduction, and tirzepatide 10 and 15 mg were superior to dulaglutide 1.5 mg in body weight reduction. Similar to the tirzepatide phase 1 study ([18](#B18)), most of the tirzepatide adverse events (AEs) were GI-related, with the incidence higher than that for the GLP-1 receptor agonist class. Additionally, a placebo-contr\n\n[...]\n\nfficacy and safety of three doses of tirzepatide (5, 10, and 15 mg) once weekly in participants with type 2 diabetes. Based on the findings of the phase 2 studies, the dose escalation scheme in the SURPASS trials was as follows: all doses started at 2.5 mg once weekly for 4 weeks; then each dose was escalated every 4 weeks in increments of 2.5 mg until the corresponding maintenance dose of tirzepatide (5, 10, or 15 mg) was attained and maintained for the remainder of the study ([Figure 1](#F1)). Tirzepatide was compared with placebo as monotherapy in SURPASS-1 ([NCT03954834](https://clini\n\n[...]\n\nblind trials, whereas SURPASS-2, SURPASS-3, and SURPASS-4 were open-label trials. The primary efficacy end point for these studies was the change from baseline in A1C. The proportion of participants achieving an A1C value <7.0%, \u22646.5%, or <5.7% with tirzepatide across the completed SURPASS trials ranged from 81 to 97%, from 66 to 95%, and from 23 to 62%, respectively ([Table 2](#T2)). Significantly more participants achieved the three A1C values with all tirzepatide doses in each of the f\n\n[...]\n\nm initiating therapy. Therefore, broadening the treatment continuum with therapies that achieve glycemic control with weight loss effects is important. In the SURPASS trials, body weight reductions from baseline at the primary end point ranged from \u22126.2 kg (\u22126.6%) with tirzepatide 5 mg when added to insulin glargine (SURPASS-5) to \u221212.9 kg (\u221213.9%) with tirzepatide 15 mg when added to metformin with or without an SGLT2 inhibitor (SURPASS-3) ([Table 2](#T2) and [Figure 2B](#F2)). There was an overall improvement in the fasting lipid profile with tirzepatide across the SURPASS trials. Namely, there was a decrease from baseline in triglycerides (by 15.2\u221226.7%), LDL cholesterol (by 5.2\u221215.5%), and VLDL cholesterol (by 14.2\u221225.2%) and an increase from baseline in HDL cholesterol (by 0.9\u221210.8%) with all tirzepatide doses at the primary end point. Decreases in systolic (of 2.8\u221212.6 mmHg) and diastolic (of 0.8\u22124.5 mmHg) blood pressure were also observed with tirzepatide in all SURPASS trials.
